speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two with Rose Kohn. So you were in the ghetto with your mother, father, sister. And your grandmother had died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She died in the ghetto. How was she buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was buried on the cemetery. This-- that one was a Jewish cemetery there. And they just took her out in a cart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it within the confines of the ghetto, the cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah-ha. Mm-hm. Right. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did a rabbi perform her burial service?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- and now this is 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: '40. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you would have been 16 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What did you do every day, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were working, you know, every--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: --everybody was assigned to work. I was-- I-- I worked in a knitting factory-- no. It wasn't a factory. It-- it was-- they teach us, like a school. It was a school. And then we had the teachers. And the younger people came in there, the ones that knew how to knit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were knitting different things. I was knitting tablecloth on one round needle, I remember. I don't know who it went to-- or little doilies. Are you knitting or crocheting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Knitting. Knitting. Yeah. Then we when knitting those little hats for the Germans for [NON-ENGLISH], for the soldiers to cover-- cover their ears-- this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get your supplies-- the knitting wool?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Some-- that's through the-- through that border there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. They were just delivered.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything came in. There were people that were in charge of everything, you know. It was like a little government--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a little Jewish government. You know, with money?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid for your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were paid. I don't remember what. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember the currency?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: --ghetto money.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was ghetto money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto money. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what could you buy with your ghetto money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could buy-- I don't know-- a little extra something, maybe besides that ration. At the beginning-- see, I-- this is something is not very clear--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with me. It's not the-- clear how it went. 'Cause, you know, we were so sheltered. My parents weren't-- you had to have [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what that means? You have to know people so you would have a broader idea of what's going on, and get some more things. And our-- my parents didn't. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they did not. They just knew how to go to work and come back. And we never had any extras-- ever. Our family never had any extra. Where others-- other families had some good food and somehow organized--

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: --organized things.

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Those people knew how to work the system, but your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the system. That's exactly, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What about you and your sister? Were you socializing at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. We didn't do anything-- just read. We had a book here and there, and we read. And we were tired after work. Cold-- wintertime was unbearably cold-- no heating-- no heating whatsoever, you know. The-- the-- the walls were all with ice inside the walls-- icy. Everything was ice. And-- no. I don't remember socializing-- just sitting, and worrying, and talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the five of you? Or four of you now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the-- yeah-- and then meeting a few people we knew at the beginning. But later on, everybody was just involved with themselves, with fears, and hunger, and pain. And that's-- and that what it was. It wasn't-- I-- I don't know. They had a little social life too, they said. Later, people told me some theories, you know. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember doing any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all stay well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- I mean, to a certain time. In 1942, when the Germans needed a lot of people to dispose of-- so they made selections. And mine-- they stopped bringing in food to the ghetto. So people didn't get anything. And potatoes were waiting on the border there. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. The Germans said [NON-ENGLISH]. And this was our borderline. And all the food came through that place. Everything was standing there for two weeks. The potatoes got all rotten. And when this selection was over, we all-- I mean, we were ordered, all of us, to come down to the yard, line up. And they-- and they were selecting us. Whoever they want on the truck and whom they wanted to stay, put you to the right-- same thing like in Auschwitz, you know. So, of course, my mother was taken away at the selection. My father, we sent out to another street, because he was all swollen from hunger. I have a book here I-- I wanna show you-- the person I'm gonna talk about now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, they took my mother away. And me and my sister went upstairs crying, crying. We knew this is it. All of the sudden, my mother comes up to the house. I said, what happened? My mother's friend's son was the Jewish police-- in the Jewish police. And he was assigned to our apartment house to come to help the Nazis, the Germans. So what he did, he took her out from the line-- from the line of-- from the truck. He risked his life. And he put her back in the line again. And she passed the second time, OK? So-- so that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought a book in that Holocaust Museum, and here he is that wonderful guy that I'm so proud of. And I would like to show it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to you later. He is in this book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember when this selection was?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, in October-- October or September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your-- had your mother also been working when she-- was she also doing the knitting with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, my mother worked somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. But She also had a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah, yeah, yeah. My mother worked already at that time at-- with the clothes that was coming back-- all the clothes that was coming back from the people that were taken out. That's where she worked. But at--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what had to be done with those clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: To look over the diamonds, and the gold, and all. Because they knew people were sewing in things in their garments. So they were sitting there and selecting, you know. Children is separately, and adults, and probably sweaters, and all that kind-- and shoes. So-- but I was gonna tell you about this-- the potatoes. The potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they came in after it was over, this selection was over, and we were together again-- the four of us. Everybody got sick-- typhoid, diarrhea. And everybody got sick. So my sister and me, we both got very ill. And we-- we slept. I had-- I had 'dysenteria'-- 'dysen-terry'-- dysentery?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, dysentery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And hers turned out to be typhoid. And she couldn't make it. And she didn't make it. She died. And hundreds and thousands of young people were putting piles of bodies. 'Cause I went into the mortuary, and I saw piles and piles of bodies-- oh, in the thousands. But I was lucky. I just-- mine-- we drank from the same glass, you know-- both of us. And so this is when I lost my sister. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take those-- did they bury those bodies? Were you able to have a burial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, but just-- just threw them in, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like one big grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no, no, no, no, no-- separate-- separate. But just threw them in on a board.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no other things to do. And-- and-- and then my father, we didn't tell my father that she passed away for a while, because he was ill. At that time he got ill. And so he-- he suffered. In-- in 1943, in July, he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had, what do you call? TB-- TB-- and they couldn't-- nothing could be done. The doctor told us-- the Jewish-- a ghetto doctor-- if the war ends, he has a chance. And if it doesn't, so-- he can't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1943, you're saying?

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were doctors of-- of a sort with no equipment, I assume?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with no-- they couldn't do nothing. Yeah. Mm-hm. Mm-hm. And he was also in a so-called hospital. And then they send him home, and he choked to death. It was summer-- one little room, no air-- absolutely no air. It was a horrible, horrible thing to watch-- horrible for me. Anyway, so that was in '43. And-- and then, let's see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then it was just you and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my mother. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continue to work? Were you're still working all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was still working. I worked at another-- I worked at this-- it was called, in German, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]. This was a place where we used to pack the parts of-- to warm up the engines for the planes-- so that part. And we worked in a-- in a-- I would packing. I was putting in, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Assembly line, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Assembly line-- assembly line, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when you did that, who were the overseers there? Were they Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish people everywhere. Everything was done by Jewish people. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you about these people, these Jews who were in charge of other Jews. What kind of people where they? What was the treatment like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some were very kind. Some were nice. And some were not. Because everybody wanted to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they stole all the food they could, you know, and they-- for them to have it good. So the other would get very little. It was stealing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even that Rumkowski-- Rumkowski was our-- our-- the eldest of the juden. And he always appealed to the Jews-- don't steal. Don't take. Well, he took himself. But-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --but that's in times like that. What are you gonna do? Everybody wants to survive on-- on an account of another. They just-- so-- Do you have any idea how many people were in the ghetto? At-- at-- at the point at which the most people were there, did you have any idea of how many you were talking about? How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 150,000, 130,000, I would say. A lot of people came from Germany. You know, they brought in Germans and Czechoslovakian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came in, and I felt so sorry when I saw them coming. I said, oh my God. Where are you coming to?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, 'cause we already had our treatment. And they came. They look so good. And they had all the beautiful clothes on. And my heart was going out for these people. I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You didn't resent the fact that they'd had it better than you for a couple years already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: At the point of which-- and when your mother-- I mean, your father and your sister died, after the selections had been made, how many people then? What was the sense of it? I-- I know you-- you didn't have a number, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have the feeling like 1/2 of the people were gone? Or did it feel very empty to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't empty. We still had-- because of these people that came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --kind of. But-- but I think we had 80,000, while-- before the big-- the big-- before they closed the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they brought out about 80,000. 700 supposed to be saved, I mean, that's in the books--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I mean, that they write about.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you worked-- I'd like to get back to your working in this-- not exactly munitions factory--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --but this airplane factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had Jewish supervisors-- foremen. Do you remember any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- or any experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Nothing. No, I just went to work, and-- I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it's just you and your mom in that room. Is it the same room--

speaker: interviewee
text: And no more work--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and no more--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no more work-- after '43, no. I don't think we were working. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that make it better or worse not to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was worse, because they-- they give you that little food. What you got was hardly enough to survive. And people that worked still had some extra money to buy extra. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of trying to escape the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wasn't that type. [LAUGHTER] No. No, I never-- well, you couldn't-- a large ghetto, you couldn't. You could never. It was too watched-over. I mean, very seldom, I don't think any-- you approached it only-- you know, you-- you got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You close to that border.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the gate-- to the fence-- and the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the gate it was-- it was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Electrified?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Upstairs-- I mean, what you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh yeah. I know. The bridge that went-- right.

speaker: interviewee
text: A bridge-- a bridge-- a bridge, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now, in 1943, you've lost your father. And there was no more work you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so. I-- I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: --working. I just don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? What happened after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day after day, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --you know, we had neighbors. And we visited and talked to neighbors, you know-- and hoping that it's going to get better. You know, and then people gave up. I mean, nobody--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was positive, you know? Nobody was positive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long before you were selected out? How long did you stay in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the ghetto till 1944, a year later. It was like 11 months, you know, after my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then it would have been like June or April?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it was July-- I think the beginning of July. That's what I think it was, July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there was supposed-- when-- in-- in 1944, there was selections were taking place every few months. And we all had to be in it-- all of us. They said, if they find you, they're going to kill you-- if they find you in a-- in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they also, in-- in-- in '43, they took away my aunt, my uncle, and my cousins-- young cousins, you know, and my mother's brothers. The whole family went. And we didn't know where they did-- where they took them. But they were taken out. So-- Had you stayed in touch with them in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, yeah. But nobody was that close. I mean, it's just-- I often think about it. Nobody-- everybody was just busy with their own--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: --serious, you know, survival and hunger, you know. I mean, even when my father died, nobody came. Me and my mother-- that's the only thing-- we went to the funeral, you know? But then, it's 1944, and my sister's girlfriend begs me to go with, because they were selected to go-- to be sent out from the ghetto. You know, they promise-- the promises were that they're going to-- you going to work. You're gonna have it good. You're gonna-- so they were excited, you know-- some of the people I knew. And she said, Rose, you must go with us. I have a mother, you have a mother. We have to go, you know. And by that time, my mother was selected to go too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had been selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had been selected, yeah-- to go-- to go too. And I was hiding. I was hiding out in my neighbor's house. But I told you about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I need you to tell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. So I was-- I was. And by the time the selection was over, I come down to my room, and my mother is not there. So I knew already, you know, where she is. And the same policeman-- our friend, came over. And he told me. You know, your mother was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, you know what? I'm gonna take my-- I took my little-- what do you call-- back-- backpack, and I ran to that-- to the wagons where all the Jews were in already. And I-- the German, the Nazi, called each-- each compartment to see where my mother is so I could be with her together. When you're talking about wagons and compartments, are they-- are you talking about the train already? Or were these the carts taking them to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The train. The train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- so they-- she was already at the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was already at the train station. But what I was gonna say-- I'm-- I'm mixing a little up now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm mixing a little. What happened is that-- that-- that a girl asked me to go, and I did not. So my little ration card and mother's was taken away. Because we did not come at that time to the place to be taken away. So they did not feed us at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- this--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're talking your friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was with--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was before--

speaker: interviewee
text: --when the friend--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your mother was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my friend, when she begged me to go with her-- right? OK. So I-- and my intuition said no. You're not going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was hiding for two weeks-- mother and me. Mother went at night somewhere else. I went somewhere else, you know, wherever we could. And-- because the Jewish police was looking for you. See, they had your names. And-- and I told her, no. I'm not going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she did go. And after the war, I found out where they were taken-- to Chelmno. Everybody was-- everybody was finished up there-- finished off there. And my intuition said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the last day I couldn't anymore. Almost, I almost was ready to go. And all of the sudden, I hear voices in the streets. People are so happy. They stopped the selections. They are not taking people out anymore. You know? So it was great.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened, they decided to take everybody. They decided to close the ghetto. And this is when, you know, I was saved. And in Auschwitz, at least you had a choice-- a chance. I had a chance. So here I am-- and my mother too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother was selected. You were still in hiding, but then you ran down to look for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: To look. This was-- this was two weeks before--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when the others left. The others--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Two weeks after the others had left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. I did not go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my intuition told me, you are not going. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went down, and you were put in a train with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a compartment? What kind of a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just was one box, one box train, you know, with who knows how many people. We couldn't-- we had to stand. You couldn't even sit. And with the buckets-- the buckets of-- of all the waste right there, coming in, out, and out, and in. And it was-- I mean, we still didn't know where we going. We still--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stay there a long time or take off right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took off. It didn't stay long. It took off, yeah. It took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I wouldn't know that. I don't know. I don't know, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hours or days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- not days-- no, no, no-- hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hours. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, hours. And-- and when I arrived there, and I looked out through a hole, it was a funny way how I felt. And I said to my mother, this is not for us-- when we arrived at Auschwitz. I looked out, and I saw people, you know-- dark black. I thought they're gypsies, because they sent in gypsies to the ghetto one time-- at one time-- not with us, but adjacent to us. They send them in, and-- and then they said that they took them out to kill. This is-- that-- those were the rumors at the time. And so I said, oh. These are the gypsies here. It's not for us. We're not going out here. Look at that. This is not a place for us. And sure enough, a second later, the doors open like a-- like-- like an-- ugh-- with such a bang-- and the screams, and the yells. [GERMAN]! Out, out, out! And you couldn't even concentrate where you are and what's going on. By the time I knew, I was walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what happened, you know. You just couldn't-- couldn't understand what's going on. So I was walking. We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hang on-- hung onto your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, yeah. She was lucky. She was lucky at this-- this time too. And-- and we came in to this place where they were shaving us. And they were, you know, bathing, us, and giving you, you know, like one-- one person got the top of a pajama, the other person got the bottom of the pajama. You know, and this took till late at night when it was dark. And we came in, and-- and then they let us go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wouldn't know where I'm going and what we are doing. Everybody's crying. Nobody recognizes anybody anymore, because all the hair is gone, you know? And people change. They see environments like that, they-- you-- so a sister couldn't recognize a sister. And they brought us into one place. It was dark. And I touched, and I touched, and I touched. Where are we? So I found a hole. And I touched again, and there's another hole. So later on I learned this was the bathroom for 1,000 people. They had this bathroom, the holes, for so many people to come in. And there they kept us for a little while until they brought us in to the barracks there. So there was a-- a girl, you know, she was a neighbor of mine. And I knew her. But she has been-- had been there already for two years. And everybody cries, where are we? What-- she said, don't ask questions. I said-- she said to us, you are the lucky ones. The others, you see that-- you see that smoke? That's what happened to the others. You know, you talk about it-- it was at night and it was so dark. When you got to the barracks, were there any lights there at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I didn't see any lights. I don't-- I don't remember how long we stayed in this one and when-- when they brought us out. This, I don't remember it through the barracks. And so we-- we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rose, do you remember the selection process at all? I know you said there was so much confusion and chaos. Do you remember actually being sent to the right, to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to the right. I mean, it was in such a manner. I still see the guy, the Nazi. You know, I don't know who he was. But he just pushed me down there and pushed my mother down there, you know, in such a hurry, in such a-- they were so nervous themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I don't know nervous. I wouldn't-- I-- I don't-- I can't say nervous. But to do it so fast-- to do it so fast-- they-- it-- it was a hell hole there, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- and then we were at the barracks, you know. And we learned. And here you got the little spoon. And you got the little dish to hold onto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and here I meet my two friends. They were in another barrack. And I said to them like, why don't you join me? I said, you come move with so we'll be together-- so naive, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I still think about that, you know-- when I told them. And we talk about it. One is still alive, you know. She lives in Sweden. And-- [LAUGHS] --come and join us. We'll be together. Because we grew up together in Poland, you know. We're close. And then-- and then that was-- Birkenau, actually. They took us to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only there for two weeks-- two weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of terror-- terror. Well, look. We-- we sit at these-- on these bunks-- eight people on this small little thing. And they would throw at us one dish of soup for seven-- for seven, for eight, whatever. I still remember that. And-- and you couldn't move to the bathroom. You have to wait till they were ready to take you. And then early in the morning, you stood on appell, and-- or you had to knee down, knell--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kneel down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kneel down. Kneel down, yeah, for hours in the mud, in the dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year? What season?

speaker: interviewee
text: Season? It was in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: July. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was hot, probably, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hot, yeah-- and-- and thirst-- people were thirsty. I was thirsty. I couldn't eat. I was so hungry in the ghetto. And over there, they gave you a nice piece of bread, a nice piece of sausage. And I just couldn't eat. I did not eat. I couldn't eat. I stopped eating-- only water, water, water.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who could get the water? Somebody came by with a bucket of water. Everybody was grabbing. So 1/2 was going to waste, because everybody wanted water. It was so hot. This was a horrible time. So it was two weeks like that. All right. Hold on. We're gonna change the tape. [SIGHS]
